summary: Gruber offers a wide-ranging roundup of observations from the 2008 Macworld Expo keynote. He notes Apple's conspicuous snubbing of Microsoft's Office 2008 demo, praises Time Capsule while questioning whether Apple killed third-party backup competition, and examines the curious $20 iPod Touch upgrade fee through the lens of Sarbanes-Oxley accounting. He covers Apple TV 2.0's content challenges, the MacBook Air's novel multi-touch trackpad gestures, and Randy Newman's provocative anti-Bush performance. Throughout, he balances genuine enthusiasm for Apple's products with pointed skepticism about some of their business decisions.
key_points: Apple deliberately downplayed Microsoft Office 2008 at the keynote, reflecting growing confidence and competitive tension with iWork
Time Capsule is a great product, but Apple may have killed third-party AirPort disk backup support to eliminate competition — a move Gruber calls 'shitty, pure and simple' if true
The $20 iPod Touch upgrade fee appears to be an opportunistic charge rather than a strict Sarbanes-Oxley necessity, especially since the update is only 9 kilobytes
Apple is taking a Microsoft-like dogged approach to Apple TV, iterating through versions despite early failures, with content licensing being the real constraint
The MacBook Air's multi-touch trackpad gestures are promising but limited by the lack of third-party app support at launch
money_quotes: I think you can really make an argument that Time Machine is the most important feature Apple has added to Mac OS X in years, maybe ever, and support for doing it over the network makes it better.
But if Apple has pulled support for hard drive/base station backups to eliminate Time Capsule competition, that's shitty, pure and simple.
I'm left with the feeling that they're charging $20 for the iPod Touch upgrade simply because they can.
Apparently the apps are already there on the 1.1.3 Touch OS, because the upgrade itself weighs in at just 9 kilobytes.
The big problem Apple faces with Apple TV isn't technical — it's content. They're constrained by what the TV networks and movie studios will allow them to do.
It's certainly hard to imagine any other major corporation in the U.S. that would invite Randy Newman on stage to perform a song like that.
There were audible groans in the keynote hall when Jobs announced that the iPod Touch update costs $20.
key_insight: Even when praising Apple's products, Gruber holds the company accountable for business decisions that prioritize profit over customer trust — particularly when features are artificially restricted to sell proprietary hardware.
